Member-only story

JavaScript isn’t difficult because of its syntax. It’s difficult because production exposes assumptions that local development never challenges.

7 min read
4 hours ago

--

Most of the bugs I’ve spent days chasing weren’t caused by JavaScript being confusing. They came from believing that code which survived local testing had already earned the right to run in production.
One Tuesday morning, an API that had been stable for months started returning duplicate records. Nothing had changed in the deployment. CPU usage looked normal. Memory wasn’t climbing. The logs showed every request succeeding, yet customers were reporting inconsistent data.
The first instinct was to blame the database. Then the cache. Then the load balancer. Hours disappeared proving each of them innocent.
The problem lived in twenty lines of JavaScript that nobody had looked at since the feature shipped.
That experience changed how I think about the language. JavaScript isn’t difficult because its syntax is unusual. It’s difficult because production environments expose assumptions that local development quietly protects.
Most developers don’t struggle with JavaScript itself. They struggle with the gap between…
